one_line_takeaway: The first survey to systematize membership-inference attacks across both LLMs and multimodal models and across the whole pipeline (pre-training, fine-tuning, alignment, RAG), offering a knowledge x strategy taxonomy rather than new attacks or benchmarks.
key_finding: As a survey, its synthesized takeaways (drawn from cited works, not from the authors' own experiments) are: (1) MIA vulnerability generally rises with the number of trainable parameters exposed during fine-tuning [17] and analogously for LMM pre-training data under fine-tuning [35]; (2) WHERE parameters are trained can matter more than how many — head fine-tuning leaks more than full or adapter fine-tuning [44]; (3) in alignment, DPO shows higher MIA vulnerability than PPO/RLHF because preference data is more directly exposed [15]; (4) end-task fine-tuning and larger target-data granularity (document/collection scale) increase leakage [52]; (5) middle/deeper transformer layers carry more membership signal in probing attacks [27]; (6) reference/shadow-model attacks generally outperform target-model-only attacks but cost more compute and realism. Research remains heavily concentrated on transformer LLMs (Pythia, GPT family), with LMM privacy underexplored, motivating the survey's LMM expansion.
core_contribution: A survey (not a new attack or empirical study) that systematizes Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs) against large-scale models. The authors' OWN contribution is a three-axis taxonomy and the claim of being the first survey to (1) jointly cover both LLMs and Large Multimodal Models (LMMs), and (2) organize MIAs across the full model-development pipeline: pre-training, fine-tuning, model alignment (RLHF/DPO), and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). They categorize surveyed attacks by (a) targeted pipeline stage, (b) adversarial knowledge (black-box / gray-box / white-box), and (c) attack strategy (target-model-based vs. reference/shadow-model-based). A distinctive definitional contribution is refining gray-box access for the fine-tuning setting into 'practical gray-box' (partial pre-trained-model knowledge + fine-tuning API access, no fine-tuning data) and 'full gray-box' (also partial fine-tuning data). They also standardize categorization of embedding-access attacks as white-box (marked with the double-dagger symbol). Section 1 Table 1 positions the work against prior surveys ([8,26,55,31,21,49]), arguing those either omit LMMs, omit large-scale models, or predate 2024; note this comparison is Related Work, not a technical result.
threat_model: An adversary tries to determine whether a specific data point (or dataset) was part of the target model's training/retrieval data, by observing model behavior at inference. The survey frames three adversary-knowledge levels: black-box (query access, outputs, labels, auxiliary non-member data only), gray-box (additionally partial training data and/or model structure; deemed most realistic), and white-box (full weights, activations, training data, hyperparameters; treated as suitable only for internal audits since an attacker with the full training set has no need to infer membership). Stated malicious use is privacy breach of sensitive training data (e.g., inferring a patient/customer record was used by a hospital or insurer model); stated legitimate uses are auditing unauthorized data usage and pre-deployment overfitting diagnosis. Pipeline-specific stakes: fine-tuning MIA exposes user/proprietary task data; alignment MIA exposes human-preference feedback; RAG MIA exposes documents in the retrieval database (IP leakage).
limitations: Being a survey, it contributes no new attack, no reproduced benchmarks, and no head-to-head empirical comparison, so relative attack effectiveness is described qualitatively rather than measured under a common protocol. Its summary tables report categorical attributes (target models, datasets, scenario, year) but not comparable quantitative metrics (e.g., AUC/TPR@low-FPR) across attacks, limiting the reader's ability to rank methods. The paper acknowledges some studies label their setting black-box when it is effectively gray-box, a taxonomy hazard it flags but cannot fully resolve. LMM coverage, though a headline, rests on relatively few primary works ([22,23,35,56,27,30]). Future-direction and conclusion sections (Sec. 7-8) fall beyond the 22-page extraction used here.

datasets_used: No datasets are used by the survey itself (no experiments). Datasets appearing in its summary Tables 6-9 as used by the SURVEYED primary works: WikiMIA, MIMIR, PILE, OpenWebText, RedPajama-Data, WikiText-103, AG News, XSum, Wikipedia
quantitative_results: The survey reports NO quantitative results of its own (it runs no experiments); Tables 6-9 are categorical summaries (attack, targets, method, scenario, year).

Only concrete numeric decision rule quoted from a cited work: LUMIA [27] infers membership when a per-layer linear probe reaches AUC > 0.6, taking the max-AUC (often middle/deeper) layer as the decision point.
Attributed qualitative trend (not a measured number by the survey): MIA vulnerability increases with the count of trainable parameters during fine-tuning [17] and for LMM pre-training data [35].
baselines_compared: None — this is a literature survey, not a comparative empirical study, so no baselines are run. The only comparison table is Table 1, which contrasts the survey's SCOPE against six prior surveys ([8], [26], [55], [31], [21], [49]) on axes LLM/LMM/MIA-focus/pipeline-coverage/survey-cutoff, not attack performance.
